name: Mezuzah
longDescription: The mezuzah is a small parchment scroll inscribed with Torah verses, rolled and placed inside a protective case, then affixed to the doorposts of Jewish homes. The word "mezuzah" literally means "doorpost" in Hebrew, though common usage has transferred the term to the scroll and case mounted upon it. This practice transforms every doorway into a point of encounter with divine commandment.

sources.name: Deuteronomy 6:4-9
sources.description: The biblical text containing the Shema and V'ahavta, the verses inscribed on the mezuzah parchment.
sources.title: Deuteronomy 6:4-9

sources.name: Talmud, Menachot 32b-33a
sources.description: A section of the Talmud discussing the laws and customs related to the mezuzah.
sources.title: Talmud, Menachot 32b-33a

sources.name: Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh De'ah 285-291
sources.description: A section of the Shulchan Aruch (Code of Jewish Law) detailing the specific laws and regulations regarding mezuzah.
sources.title: Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh De'ah 285-291

sources.name: Oral tradition and rabbinic commentaries
sources.description: Various interpretations and explanations of the mezuzah tradition passed down through generations.
sources.title: Oral tradition and rabbinic commentaries

description: The mezuzah is a small parchment scroll inscribed with Torah verses, rolled and placed inside a protective case, then affixed to the doorposts of Jewish homes. The word "mezuzah" literally means "door

keyMyths.title: Protection from Harm
keyMyths.description: The mezuzah is believed to offer divine protection to the inhabitants of the home, shielding them from harm and misfortune. It acts as a constant reminder of God's presence and covenant, fostering a sense of security and spiritual well-being.

keyMyths.title: Reminder of God's Commandments
keyMyths.description: The verses inscribed on the parchment within the mezuzah contain the Shema, a declaration of God's oneness, and the V'ahavta, a commandment to love God with all one's heart, soul, and might. Affixing the mezuzah serves as a constant visual reminder to uphold these core tenets of Jewish faith.

keyMyths.title: Symbol of Jewish Identity
keyMyths.description: The mezuzah is a visible symbol of Jewish identity and a declaration of faith to the outside world. It distinguishes a Jewish home and serves as a welcoming sign to other Jews, fostering a sense of community and belonging.

keyMyths.title: Blessing for the Home
keyMyths.description: The act of affixing a mezuzah is accompanied by a blessing, invoking God's favor upon the home and its inhabitants. This blessing asks for prosperity, health, and happiness for all who dwell within.

keyMyths.title: Continuity of Tradition
keyMyths.description: The practice of affixing a mezuzah is a longstanding tradition passed down through generations, connecting contemporary Jews to their ancestors and the rich history of Jewish observance. It reinforces the enduring nature of the covenant between God and the Jewish people.
